topic: reflecting on growth — from Kingston bedroom producer to Rebel residency

turns.content: I'm in a reflective mood tonight. just sitting in the lab looking at all this gear and thinking about how far I've come. like I used to make beats on a cracked laptop with headphones that had one working ear and now?? NOW?? the growth is unreal

That's a really meaningful moment -- being able to sit back and actually recognize your own growth. A lot of people are so busy grinding they never stop to appreciate the distance they've covered. What feels like the biggest change from those early days to where you are now?

honestly?? the CONFIDENCE. back in the day I was making beats in my room and I wouldn't even let anyone hear them!! I'd make something, think it was garbage, delete it, start over. for MONTHS. I was so scared of judgment. now I'm sending beats to people, performing for crowds, like the whole ting changed

That shift from creating in secret to putting yourself out there is one of the hardest things any artist goes through. What changed for you? Was there a specific moment where you decided to stop deleting and start sharing?

yeah actually!! I remember I made this dancehall remix in my room right, and I was about to delete it like usual. but my cousin was visiting and she heard it through the wall and came in like 'YO WHAT IS THAT PLAY IT AGAIN' and I was so embarrassed but she was genuinely HYPED. she made me send it to her and she played it at a party and people went CRAZY. that was the first time I realized maybe I'm not garbage??

That's such a great origin story! Your cousin literally saved a track from the recycle bin and it ended up moving a whole party. Sometimes we need someone else to show us what we can't see in ourselves.

It's also proof that the gap between "nobody has heard my music" and "people love my music" is often just one person pressing play. You had the talent the whole time -- you just needed that first external validation to believe it.

EXACTLY!! and from there I started posting stuff online and getting feedback from other producers and it just snowballed. like every little win gave me confidence for the next ting. first time someone asked ME for a beat. first time I played a live set. first time I got paid for my music. each one felt like a level up in a video game lol

And each level prepares you for the next one. The person who was deleting beats in secret could never have handled performing live -- you needed those intermediate steps. Getting feedback online, then selling a beat, then playing a small gig, then bigger gigs. Each step built the muscle for the next one.

What level do you feel like you're on now? And what does the next level look like for you?

right now I feel like I'm at the level where the FOUNDATION is solid. like I have a studio, I have regular gigs, I have a workflow that works for me, people in the scene know my name. but the next level?? the next level is BIGGER. I want my productions on other people's albums. I want to tour. I want my sound to be recognized not just in Toronto but INTERNATIONALLY. Caribbean diaspora worldwide should know my riddim

That's a beautiful vision and honestly? You've already proven you can level up because you've done it multiple times. Going from bedroom producer to having a studio and regular gigs IS the proof of concept. The blueprint is the same for going international -- it's just a bigger scale.

And "Caribbean diaspora worldwide" is a massive but very real audience. The global appetite for dancehall-influenced, Caribbean-rooted electronic music is growing fast. Producers who can bridge that sound with modern production are in demand.

What do you think is the main thing standing between where you are now and that international recognition?

honestly I think it's VISIBILITY. my music is good enough -- I know that now. but there are so many talented producers out there who nobody knows because they're just in the lab making fire and not getting it in front of the right people. the music industry is like 30% talent and 70% making sure people HEAR you

That's a really mature perspective, especially coming from someone who started out being scared to share anything. You've gone from "I'm scared to let anyone hear this" to "my music is good enough, I just need more ears on it." That's massive growth in itself.

And you're right about the visibility game. The good news is you already know how to build an audience -- you did it locally. The international version is the same playbook: consistent output, strategic collaborations, placing your sound where new audiences can discover it. Streaming playlists, collaborating with artists from different countries, getting your music into DJ sets outside of Canada.

The bedroom producer version of you would never believe where you are right now. Don't forget that.

yo you're gonna make me CRY in the lab rn lol!! but for real though sometimes I forget to appreciate the journey because I'm so focused on the destination. tonight I just needed to sit with it and be like... yeah. I DID this. from one working headphone to a whole studio. the riddim of my life is building to something BIG and I can feel it

And you SHOULD sit with it! Gratitude for how far you've come and hunger for where you're going aren't opposites -- they fuel each other. The fact that you can feel something big building means you're paying attention.

Keep this reflective energy too. Come back to this feeling when the grind gets hard, when a session isn't going well, when you're tired and wondering if it's worth it. It IS worth it. You've got the receipts to prove it. Now go build that next level -- the world needs to hear your riddim.
